content:
Hi All!

I need to implement circular references in an object hierarchy. In user space Rust I would have used alloc::sync::Weak for back references and be done. Since we don't have that in kernel, what is my best option?

I was thinking of having a list of Arc<Revocable<SelfRef>> in the parent struct and had those out to child objects in my graph. It seems a bit cumbersome. Is that the way to go?

Or should we implement weak references for Arc. Is there a reason for the Arc not to implement weak references? Should we have another reference counted pointer that implements weak references?
#Tyr > Should we consider a Weak<T> type?
What I do in Binder is that when a process is cleaned up (because you closed the fd), then as part of the other cleanup tasks, I also break cycles.

So in the BinderProcess struct, there's a collection of BinderThread objects, and each BinderThread has a ref to the BinderProcess. During process cleanup, the BinderThread collection is destroyed. This way there are no cyclic refs after cleanup.
If we want a Weak, then I think it should be a separate type from Arc. There can be a separate StrongArc type.
Thanks for the pointers, I'll take a look :+1:
What is the rationale for not having weak refs in current Arc. Is it performance?

I mean, on that front I care more about additional memory usage than runtime performance.

But another factor is that, at least if we wish to use the same implementation as stdlib, then we would not be able to use the kernel's refcount_t anymore.
Right. I assume we would write an implementation ourselves at any rate. But not allocating the extra word when we don't need makes sense.
I see that you also implemented weak references in your NodeRef. That code could probably leverage a shared StrongArc type?
(Very awesome to be able to find binder in linux-next by the way)
I don't think StrongArc would be suitable for NodeRef.
The kernel-level objects need to exist even if it's only weak.
The part that gets deleted when there are only weak refs is a userspace object.
NodeRef keeps track of the count that the kernel holds on the userspace object.
OK, got it. I was just randomly reading code and came across that.

The simplest possible implementation of Weak would be to just use two refcount_ts, one for the weak and one for the strong count. But I don't think methods such as into_unique_or_drop() can be correctly implemented in that case - there are some pretty annoying races you have to handle which is why stdlib does something more complex.
